momentum-agent-composition-pipeline.name: Agent Composition Pipeline — Constitution + Manifesto + Base Body
momentum-agent-composition-pipeline.description: The pipeline that produces project-conditioned agent prompts. Composes plugin base bodies + project-wide constitution (Tier 1) + per-agent manifesto (Tier 2 — role × domain routing and permissions) into the system prompt that spawns each subagent.
momentum-agent-composition-pipeline.value_analysis:
Current value: constitution-builder skill exists today and generates ## Permissions + ## Standing Rules + ## Quick Routing sections for KB-backed agent skills. The wiki KB suite is installed (DEC-018) and wiki-query is the standard Tier 3 access interface. The hand-rolled frontend-dev skill demonstrates the composition shape — a dev + frontend-stack agent with TDD/MVI rules and stack tooling baked in.

Full vision: Three-tier architecture per DEC-001 and DEC-008 D1. Tier 1 is the Hot Constitution — project-wide, shared by every agent, always loaded; contains cross-cutting rules, permissions, and the wiki-query interface block. Generated by reworked constitution-builder (domain knowledge only — routing is NOT here). Tier 2 is the Composed Agent File — per-agent (role × domain), formed as base_body + constitution + manifesto; the manifesto carries the agent-specific routing entries and per-agent permissions. Generated by manifesto-builder (new skill). Tier 3 is the Cold KB — Obsidian wiki vault, accessed on-demand via wiki-query. build-guidelines orchestrates both constitution-builder and manifesto-builder so a single skill invocation produces the full composition for a project. Sprint-dev resolves the composed Tier 2 file at spawn time and supplies it as the spawned agent's system prompt.

Known gaps: Three tracked intake stubs from 2026-05-14 capture the immediate critical path — iq-20260514-constitution-builder-rework-domain-knowledge-only, iq-20260514-manifesto-builder-new-skill, iq-20260514-tier2-doc-reconciliation. wiki-query interface block needs to land in the hot constitution. agent-manifesto-format-specification (renamed from project-manifest) defines the per-agent manifesto file format. agents-md-manifest-format declares per-skill role slots. constitution-builder write-mode parameterization, skills-kb-query injection audit, build-guidelines orchestration update, and sprint-dev/sprint-planning composed-file wiring are all backlog. The earlier-considered direction to drop build-guidelines entirely has been superseded — build-guidelines survives as the orchestrator of constitution-builder + manifesto-builder.
momentum-agent-composition-pipeline.system_context: The project-knowledge layer. Pairs with agent-role-contracts (the role contracts that get conditioned) and practice-knowledge-base (the wiki KB that wiki-query reads). Without this pipeline, every spawned subagent operates with generic role-contract knowledge only — wrong stack, wrong testing framework, wrong architecture decisions.
acceptance_conditions: A developer can run build-guidelines on a project and observe that one project-wide constitution is produced AND one manifesto per (role × domain) is produced, with sprint-dev composing all three (base body + constitution + manifesto) into each spawned subagent's system prompt.
momentum-agent-composition-pipeline.notes: Split out from momentum-composable-specialist-agents on 2026-05-14. Renamed from momentum-agent-constitution-integration after architecture clarification: composition formula is agent = base_body + constitution + manifesto, where constitution is project-wide (one) and manifesto is per role × domain (many). Three intake-queue stubs (iq-20260514-*) await create-story promotion before joining this feature's stories list.

momentum-assessment-decision-pipeline.name: Assessment → Decision → Story Traceability Pipeline
momentum-assessment-decision-pipeline.description: The SDLC artifact traceability flow: a product state assessment (momentum:assessment) produces an assessment document; momentum:decision captures architectural decisions from that assessment into decision documents; momentum:create-story generates stories with derives_from frontmatter linking back to decisions. Every story has a traceable lineage to a decision and an assessment.
momentum-assessment-decision-pipeline.value_analysis:
Teams and developers build the wrong things — not because they're careless but because context gets lost. A decision made 6 months ago that seemed obvious then is inscrutable now. A story whose origin is unclear gets deprioritized or misimplemented.

With a complete pipeline, every story traces to a decision, every decision traces to an assessment, and every assessment documents the evidence that drove it. This is not 'traceability for audits' — it is the knowledge system that makes a solo developer with an AI collaborator as strategically coherent as a team with a product manager and engineering lead. The AI always knows why a thing was built, not just what it is.

Currently, assessment and decision skills work in practice. Architecture decisions are tracked in architecture.md. The formal assessment→decision→story trace is used (e.g., feature-artifact-schema derives_from DEC-002). However, end-to-end navigation is not yet fully automated.

Gaps: derives-from frontmatter is used but not yet enforced. The provenance scanner (separate feature) would make the chain navigable automatically.
momentum-assessment-decision-pipeline.system_context: Provides the intellectual scaffolding for all feature and sprint decisions. Without it, the practice produces output but not direction. Partners with feature-grooming: features are the value; the assessment-decision pipeline is the rationale for why we build each one.
acceptance_conditions: A developer can run momentum:assessment, then momentum:decision, then momentum:create-story and produce a story whose derives_from frontmatter points to the decision document, which in turn references the assessment that motivated it.
momentum-assessment-decision-pipeline.notes: Skills exist and are working. derives_from frontmatter is used in practice. Formal traceability validation (provenance scanner) is a separate feature — hence partial, not working.

momentum-backlog-refinement.name: Backlog Refinement and Epic Taxonomy
momentum-backlog-refinement.description: The optional Momentum cycle steps that keep the backlog sprint-ready: triage (classify incoming observations), intake (capture story stubs), feature-grooming (feature taxonomy maintenance), epic-grooming (epic-level coverage review), and refine (backlog drift detection and reprioritization). Each is an optional step in the cycle sequence (triage → intake → feature-grooming → epic-grooming → refine → sprint-planning → sprint-dev → retro) that can be run between retro and sprint-planning to ensure a clean, well-prioritized sprint input.
momentum-backlog-refinement.value_analysis:
The backlog is the queue from which all sprint value flows. A messy backlog — duplicated stories, incorrectly tagged epics, gaps in coverage — produces bad sprints. The optional cycle steps are foundational: the quality of the backlog directly determines the quality of sprint planning.

The value is not primarily pain removal but structural integrity. A developer with a clean, well-organized backlog starts every sprint planning session with a reliable input. Epic-grooming gives the developer an outside-in view of whether the backlog actually covers the product vision — surfacing gaps not visible from inside individual stories. Feature-grooming maintains the feature taxonomy that orients sprint goals. Refine catches drift between story state and reality before it contaminates a sprint.

Currently working: epic-grooming, refine-skill, and reprioritization are operational. Triage is complete. The backlog-add command is the only remaining gap.

Gaps: Backlog-add is incomplete. Coverage analysis and deduplication are partial.
momentum-backlog-refinement.system_context: Feeds sprint-planning with ready stories. Without backlog hygiene, sprint-planning is selecting from a noisy, partially-valid input — and the resulting sprint reflects that noise. This feature is the upstream dependency for sprint planning quality.
acceptance_conditions: A developer can run momentum:epic-grooming and receive an outside-in view of whether the backlog covers the product vision, with gaps identified and story stub recommendations. A developer can run momentum:refine and receive a reprioritized backlog with duplicate stories flagged.
momentum-backlog-refinement.notes: Epic-grooming and refine-skill operational. Backlog-add, coverage-analysis-deduplication, and triage-skill are backlog.

momentum-feature-taxonomy-maintenance.name: Feature Taxonomy Maintenance — Schema, Grooming, Breakdown
momentum-feature-taxonomy-maintenance.description: The write layer for features.json: feature-artifact-schema defines the shape, /momentum:feature-grooming maintains the taxonomy holistically (six-signal scan: MERGE/SPLIT/DEDUP/NEW/RETIRE/UPDATE), and /momentum:feature-breakdown enumerates the stories needed to ship a feature end-to-end and dispatches them to /momentum:triage. Together they keep features.json honest while the canvas displays it.
momentum-feature-taxonomy-maintenance.value_analysis:
A developer keeps features.json honest by running /momentum:feature-grooming (holistic taxonomy maintenance with MERGE/SPLIT/DEDUP/NEW/RETIRE/UPDATE signals against the existing set) and /momentum:feature-breakdown (enumerate the stories needed to ship a feature end-to-end, dispatch to triage). The schema itself — value_analysis, system_context, type taxonomy, acceptance_condition — is defined by the feature-artifact-schema story already shipped.

The deeper value is that the feature taxonomy stays current with how the developer is actually thinking about the product. Without grooming, features.json drifts: scope creeps, redundancies accumulate, capability gaps go un-noticed. Without feature-breakdown, 'I need to ship feature X' stays a vague aspiration instead of a concrete list of stories ready for triage. Together they make the feature layer a living artifact — the canvas can only display what this layer maintains.

Known gaps: feature-grooming and feature-breakdown both work today and have shipped foundation stories. One improvement is already enumerated as backlog — feature-breakdown-fixture-enumeration-recall (recall benchmarking for the breakdown skill's enumeration completeness). Other improvements (gap-analysis enrichment, dependency-aware breakdown, cross-feature consistency checks) are not yet enumerated; feature-breakdown will surface them when run on this feature.
momentum-feature-taxonomy-maintenance.system_context: The write layer for features.json. Produces the data the canvas Features lens displays. Pairs with momentum-assessment-decision-pipeline (decisions are the rationale for which features to maintain) and momentum-backlog-refinement (story stubs from feature-breakdown flow into backlog refinement via triage).
acceptance_conditions: A developer can run /momentum:feature-grooming to detect signals across the existing feature set and update features.json with explicit approval, and can run /momentum:feature-breakdown <feature_slug> to enumerate the stories needed to ship that feature end-to-end and dispatch them to /momentum:triage.
momentum-feature-taxonomy-maintenance.notes: Carved from former momentum-feature-status-visibility split. Three foundation stories done plus one backlog story (feature-breakdown-fixture-enumeration-recall) just reassigned from momentum-backlog-refinement. Future improvements to grooming/breakdown will land here as feature-breakdown enumerates them.

momentum-impetus-experience.name: Impetus: Session Host and Practice Companion
momentum-impetus-experience.description: The full Impetus UX experience: personality, identity, visual design (ASCII art, nerdfont icons), session hosting behavior, and the emotional/motivational quality of the session opening. Distinct from session orientation (what information is shown) — this feature is about how it feels to open Momentum.
momentum-impetus-experience.value_analysis:
Impetus is more than a greeter — he is the personality of the Momentum practice. When a developer opens Claude Code, Impetus is their first contact: he should know where they left off, what's in flight, what's been accomplished, and what to do next. He should feel like a collaborator.

Right now, Impetus provides functional orientation and the greeting is fast (startup cache just shipped). But the full vision is considerably richer: ASCII art, nerdfont icons, a voice that makes the developer want to start work. A developer who feels welcomed, oriented, and energized is in a different mental state than one who reads a config dump. That difference compounds across hundreds of sessions.

The developer has described the current experience as 'a lifeless config dump.' The identity redesign stories have shipped but the full personality is not yet realized.

Gaps: Visual design layer (ASCII art, icon system) is not implemented. The greeting does not yet feel like a collaborator; it feels like a terminal tool.
momentum-impetus-experience.system_context: Impetus is the gateway to all other Momentum features. Every developer interaction starts here. A poor Impetus experience undermines confidence in the entire practice — even if everything else works well. A rich Impetus experience makes developers want to use the practice every session.
acceptance_conditions: A developer opens a Momentum session and is greeted by Impetus with a distinct personality — ASCII art header, nerdfont-icon-decorated status lines, a voice that matches the practice philosophy — such that the experience feels like opening a session with a collaborator rather than running a status command.
momentum-impetus-experience.notes: Identity foundation stories done. Full personality layer (ASCII art, visual identity) not yet implemented. Developer feedback: currently feels like a lifeless config dump.

momentum-practice-distillation.name: Lightweight Practice Artifact Distillation
momentum-practice-distillation.description: The momentum:distill skill: compresses hard-won practice knowledge into a reusable skill, rule, or agent definition in a single command. Takes a pattern identified in retrospective or session work and encodes it as a durable practice artifact.
momentum-practice-distillation.value_analysis:
Every session, every sprint, every retro produces knowledge that could make future work better. Without distillation, that knowledge lives in files and commit messages — accessible but not actionable. Distillation is the act of compressing hard-won insight into a reusable form: a skill, a rule, an agent definition.

The value is compounding returns. A developer who distills insights from each sprint finds the practice progressively smarter — less re-explanation, better defaults, fewer recurring mistakes. Over time, the practice is shaped by the developer's actual working style rather than a generic starting template.

Currently partial — the momentum:distill skill ships and is in active use. Known gaps: worktree isolation (distill currently operates on the main working tree), path classification (plugin vs personal rule detection), and jargon self-definition (distill output must define its own terms).
momentum-practice-distillation.system_context: The mechanism by which the flywheel (retro-and-flywheel) encodes patterns into the practice. Without distillation, the retro identifies patterns but they don't change anything durable. With it, insights become persistent parts of the practice.
acceptance_conditions: A developer can invoke momentum:distill with a description of a practice pattern and receive a draft skill, rule, or agent definition that encodes the pattern — committed and immediately available in the practice.
momentum-practice-distillation.notes: Distill skill working. Three improvement stories in backlog: worktree isolation, path classification redesign, jargon self-definition.

momentum-practice-flywheel.name: Practice Improvement Flywheel — Cross-Sprint Pattern Detection
momentum-practice-flywheel.description: Cross-sprint pattern detection and findings accumulation: patterns from multiple sprints surface as findings in .momentum/signals/, which feed back into sprint planning recommendations and the practice itself. The mechanism that makes each sprint better than the last.
momentum-practice-flywheel.value_analysis:
Currently no delivery — all three flywheel stories are backlog and .momentum/signals/ does not exist. The retro ceremony (momentum-sprint-retro) runs and produces per-sprint findings, but cross-sprint pattern accumulation is not implemented. A developer today gets ceremony value but not compounding practice improvement.

The full vision is transformative. A developer running sprints without the flywheel is running in place — each sprint starts from the same baseline. With the flywheel, patterns from sprint N feed into sprint N+1 as concrete recommendations: 'AC ambiguity has occurred in 4 of the last 6 sprints, consider an AC review step.' The findings ledger under .momentum/signals/ is the persistent record; the Cycle lens in Momentum Cycle (momentum-canvas) surfaces it visually. A developer who runs 10 sprints with the flywheel should be materially more effective in sprint 10 than sprint 1 — not through personal improvement but through accumulated system intelligence. This is the feature that makes Momentum a practice, not just a workflow.

All three implementation stories are backlog. The .momentum/signals/ directory does not yet exist. The flywheel stories were previously bundled with the retro ceremony in momentum-retro-and-flywheel — this split makes the gap explicit and independently trackable.
momentum-practice-flywheel.system_context: Downstream of momentum-sprint-retro (consumes per-sprint findings as raw input for pattern detection). Upstream of momentum-sprint-planning-to-ready (surfaces cross-sprint recommendations at planning time) and momentum-canvas (Cycle lens displays .momentum/signals/ findings read-only). Sole writer of .momentum/signals/. Pairs with momentum-practice-distillation — the flywheel surfaces patterns; distillation encodes them into durable practice artifacts.
acceptance_conditions: A developer can run 3+ sprints and observe that cross-sprint patterns (recurring AC ambiguity, repeated AVFL misses, systematic story quality gaps) are detected, accumulated in .momentum/signals/, and surface as recommendations in the next sprint planning session.
momentum-practice-flywheel.notes: Split from momentum-retro-and-flywheel (2026-05-03) — flywheel accumulation stories separated from ceremony. All 3 stories backlog. .momentum/signals/ directory does not yet exist.

momentum-quick-fix-workflow.name: Single-Story Tactical Workflow
momentum-quick-fix-workflow.description: The momentum:quick-fix skill: a lightweight single-story execution path that creates a story spec, spawns a dev agent, and gates the result — without requiring a full sprint declaration. Used for urgent fixes, small experiments, and out-of-sprint tactical work.
momentum-quick-fix-workflow.value_analysis:
Not every piece of work fits a sprint. An urgent bug, a small experiment, a one-off story that doesn't belong to any current sprint — these would be blocked or require heavyweight ceremony without a quick-fix path.

This feature provides a lightweight single-story path with the same quality expectations (spec-placed, gated, committed) but without sprint pipeline overhead. The value is partly practical (no need to declare a sprint for a 15-minute fix) but also about workflow integrity — developers don't bypass the practice for small work. Quick-fix keeps the quality bar consistent even for tactical tasks.

Currently works and is actively used. Intake-skill and quick-fix-skill are operational.

Gaps: Spec placement rules are still being established. Some quality gates that apply to full sprints don't yet have a defined quick-fix equivalent.
momentum-quick-fix-workflow.system_context: The escape hatch from the sprint cycle. Without this, every change requires a full sprint — making the practice feel rigid and expensive for small work. Quick-fix makes the practice usable for the full range of development activity, not just planned sprint work.
acceptance_conditions: A developer can run momentum:quick-fix with a story title, receive a spec-placed story, have it executed by a dev agent, and merge the result — without activating a sprint.
momentum-quick-fix-workflow.notes: Quick-fix and intake skills operational. Spec placement rules (quick-fix-spec-placement-rules) backlog.

momentum-sprint-orchestration.name: Sprint Execution — Concurrent Agents + Quality Gates
momentum-sprint-orchestration.description: The required core of the Momentum cycle (sprint-planning → sprint-dev → retro) and its execution engine: concurrent agent waves, merge sequencing, quality gate integration, and team composition. A developer activates a sprint and sprint-dev handles story execution in parallel waves, gates each merge, and produces a completed sprint — advancing the practice through its three required cycle steps.
momentum-sprint-orchestration.value_analysis:
This feature fundamentally changes the developer's role during a sprint. Without it, the developer is a coordinator: scheduling agent spawns, watching for merge conflicts, deciding who goes next, manually triggering reviews. With it, the developer is a strategist: they describe what to build and the system handles everything between 'sprint planned' and 'stories merged.'

A solo developer can run like a team — concurrent parallel work streams that a human coordinator could not sustain alone. 5 stories that would take 5 focused hours to coordinate take 5 focused minutes of oversight when orchestration is working. This is a new mode of working, not just pain removal.

Sprint-dev works and is actively used. Stories execute in parallel waves, quality gates run automatically, and merge sequencing is handled. The developer does not need to manage agent turn-taking.

Gaps: Agent team composition is still partially manual. Real-time scope tracking (detecting mid-sprint drift) is incomplete. The developer doesn't yet get a live view of what's in flight vs. blocked. sprint-planning and retro are the other two required cycle steps — their features are separate but this feature is the load-bearing center of every cycle.
momentum-sprint-orchestration.system_context: The core execution engine and the three required steps of every Momentum cycle. Everything between 'sprint planned' and 'sprint done' flows through this. Without it, sprint-planning produces a plan that a developer must execute manually. Paired with quality-gates-enforced, this makes 'working software at sprint end' the default outcome. Sprint state written under .momentum/sprints/ is read by the canvas Sprints lens (momentum-canvas) for the closure strip and outcome bands.
acceptance_conditions: A developer with an activated sprint can run momentum:sprint-dev and have all sprint stories executed by specialist agents in parallel waves, with AVFL + team review gates applied before each merge — without manually managing agent sequencing or merge order.
momentum-sprint-orchestration.notes: Core orchestration working. Agent team composition, scope tracking, and team-review improvements in backlog. team-review-with-teamcreate, agent-collaboration-pattern-evaluation, and create-story-fixture-change-type-classification reassigned here from momentum-composable-specialist-agents (grooming 2026-05-02) — these are orchestration/spawn patterns and quality fixtures, not composable agent architecture.

momentum-sprint-planning-to-ready.name: Sprint Planning — Backlog to Ready Sprint
momentum-sprint-planning-to-ready.description: The end-to-end sprint planning flow: developer invokes momentum:sprint-planning, the skill reads the backlog, presents synthesis-first recommendations (not a data dump), developer selects stories, AVFL validates them, Gherkin specs are generated, and the sprint is activated with a ready-for-dev story set.
momentum-sprint-planning-to-ready.value_analysis:
Sprint planning works. A developer with a backlog can run this skill and emerge with a sprint plan — stories selected, assigned to waves, ready for sprint-dev. The immediate value is real: structure and sequence that would otherwise require manual deliberation across epics, story sizes, and dependencies.

But the full vision goes beyond convenience. The skill synthesizes across the PRD, epics, and story history to produce a plan that reflects strategy, not just logistics. A developer who has been heads-down in implementation can step back, run sprint-planning, and get an outside-in view of what the next highest-leverage work is. That is augmented strategic judgment — not just time savings.

Currently working and actively used. All 6 foundation stories are done.

Gaps: The skill does not yet explain its reasoning. The developer gets a plan but not the 'why this sprint over that sprint' narrative. Sprint velocity history is not yet factored in.
momentum-sprint-planning-to-ready.system_context: The ignition for every sprint cycle. All other sprint execution features (orchestration, quality gates, retro) have no starting state without this. Depends on backlog-refinement having kept the backlog clean. Activated sprint plans under .momentum/sprints/ are read by the canvas Sprints lens (momentum-canvas) to render the active sprint header and story slate.
acceptance_conditions: A developer with populated backlog stubs can invoke momentum:sprint-planning and receive an activated sprint with validated, Gherkin-specced, ready-for-dev stories — without manually specifying story selection criteria or review flow.
momentum-sprint-planning-to-ready.notes: All 6 stories done. Feature is working for current sprint execution. Known gap: reasoning transparency and velocity-aware planning.

momentum-sprint-retro.name: Sprint Retrospective — Transcript Audit and Sprint Closure
momentum-sprint-retro.description: momentum:retro conducts a transcript-based sprint review, runs parallel auditor agents, produces a findings document, writes the sprint summary, and hands off to triage. The ceremony that closes every sprint.
momentum-sprint-retro.value_analysis:
The retro skill works and is actively used. A developer who runs it gets a structured audit of the sprint — what stories shipped, what the auditors flagged, what distillation findings apply to the practice. Sprint closure is automated: sprint marked complete, summary written, handoff to triage produced. All four ceremony stories are done and have been running in production for multiple sprints.

The ceremony is the foundation for the flywheel (momentum-practice-flywheel, separate feature). A retro that fires consistently produces the input the flywheel needs — without the ceremony running, the flywheel has nothing to compound. The ceremony is also where Tier 1 findings (immediate distillations) are applied directly, keeping the practice sharp between cycles. The transcript-based audit catches things milestone logs miss: agent behavior patterns, reasoning quality, collaboration anti-patterns.

No known gaps in the ceremony itself. The retro fires correctly, produces reliable findings, and hands off cleanly to triage. The forward-feed to cross-sprint pattern detection is the flywheel feature's responsibility.
momentum-sprint-retro.system_context: Closes the sprint loop — writes sprint summary to .momentum/sprints/, marks sprint complete, produces triage handoff. Upstream of momentum-practice-flywheel (ceremony produces raw per-sprint findings that the flywheel accumulates across cycles) and momentum-sprint-planning-to-ready (next cycle reads the sprint summary for prior-sprint context).
acceptance_conditions: A developer can run momentum:retro after a sprint and receive a findings document produced from transcript audit, a sprint-summary.md written to .momentum/sprints/, and a triage handoff with any Tier 1 findings ready to delegate.
momentum-sprint-retro.notes: Split from momentum-retro-and-flywheel (2026-05-03) — ceremony stories separated from flywheel accumulation stories. All 4 ceremony stories done. Feature is working.

momentum-canvas.name: Momentum Cycle — Three-Lens Live Dashboard
momentum-canvas.description: The /momentum:canvas skill starts a Hono+Bun server on port 3456 and opens it in a cmux browser pane — a live, navigable three-lens dashboard (Features, Sprint, Cycle timeline) with drill-down to feature detail (L2) and story detail (L3). Read-only; data polled live from .momentum/ and _bmad-output/planning-artifacts/.
momentum-canvas.value_analysis:
Two foundation stories are done: the feature-status skill and its practice-path activation. A developer today has a working momentum:feature-status skill that writes a static HTML artifact and opens it in a browser pane. That is real, usable value — a one-command snapshot of feature coverage.

The Momentum Cycle dashboard replaces the static artifact with a live Hono+Bun server (port 3456, bun --hot, no build step). Three coordinated lenses — Features (gap analysis, status badges, story fractions), Sprint (active sprint card with outcome bands: blocked/in-progress/validated), and Cycle (horizontal 7-phase timeline showing triage→planning→dev→retro with done/next-required/not-run/pending node states) — give the developer a complete picture of product state and practice health on one screen. Drill-down navigation via breadcrumb-only nav takes the developer into feature L2 reading-mode detail (value narrative, acceptance condition, stories list) and story L3 reading-mode (frontmatter, ACs, dev notes). Dark mode for scanning lenses, warm light (#faf6ec) for reading surfaces — polarity locked by surface type with a 140ms cross-fade. The canvas becomes the surface for sprint planning approval, story review, and grooming, replacing direct markdown file reads.

All six implementation stories are in backlog (Phase 1–5: dashboard shell, features lens, cycle timeline, sprint lens, feature L2, story L3). The design spec is complete — 10-pass claude.ai/design prototype, Momentum Cycle - Final.html (Pass 10). DEC-019 records the Hono+HTMX+Bun stack decision, superseding DEC-011 D2 (Vite scaffold). DEC-011 D1 (canvas rename) and D3 (state source paths under .momentum/) remain in force.
momentum-canvas.system_context: Reads, never writes — consumes data produced by momentum-feature-taxonomy-maintenance (features.json), momentum-sprint-planning-to-ready and momentum-sprint-orchestration (sprint/story state under .momentum/sprints/ and .momentum/stories/), and momentum-practice-flywheel (findings ledger at .momentum/signals/). The lightweight skill invoker replaces momentum:feature-status entirely: port-check → cmux respawn-pane bun --hot server.tsx in services pane → cmux browser open http://localhost:3456 in viewer pane.
acceptance_conditions: A developer can run /momentum:canvas and see a live dashboard at http://localhost:3456 showing all three lenses — Features with gap flags and story fractions, Sprint with the active sprint card, Cycle with the current phase timeline — and drill down into any feature or story in reading mode.
momentum-canvas.notes: DEC-019 (2026-05-03) adopts Hono+HTMX+Bun runtime stack, superseding DEC-011 D2 (Vite scaffold). DEC-011 D1 (canvas rename) and D3 (state source paths) remain in force. Design reference: /tmp/momentum-design/feature-status/project/Momentum Cycle - Final.html (Pass 10 final). Six implementation stories added to backlog 2026-05-03 via triage.

momentum-deep-research-pipeline.name: Deep Research Pipeline — Multi-Model, Date-Anchored
momentum-deep-research-pipeline.description: Multi-model deep research with date-anchored prompts, freshness windows, AVFL corpus validation — produces provenance-tracked research artifacts for strategic decisions.
momentum-deep-research-pipeline.value_analysis:
Current value: momentum:research is a working 6-phase deep-research pipeline. Phase 2 fan-outs parallel sub-question subagents (general-purpose, WebSearch); Phase 1.4 drives Gemini Deep Research via cmux-browser surface with gemini -p CLI fallback for triangulation; Phase 5 spawns a single foreground Opus synthesizer with a clean 200K context that reads raw file paths (not inline content) to produce the synthesis briefing. AVFL corpus mode validates the body of evidence across all raw outputs. Three foundation stories are done — AVFL corpus mode, momentum:research skill, Gemini deep-research automation.

Full vision: Every strategic question gets date-pinned multi-model investigation with provenance-tracked artifacts in raw/. Domain-specific freshness windows define staleness (90 days AI/LLM, 6 months tooling, 12 months standards, 24 months principles). Outdated documents are archived with reference-chain preservation. Output is corpus-validated by AVFL across all four lenses and lands in the wiki KB for retrieval. Research feeds directly into momentum:decision (decisions cite source research) and momentum:assessment (assessments cite source research).

Known gaps: Date-anchoring and primary-source directives in research-agent prompts need hardening. Document archival with reference-chain preservation is backlog. Domain-specific freshness windows are defined but not enforced. Provenance frontmatter on synthesis outputs is partial.
momentum-deep-research-pipeline.system_context: Feeds the assessment-decision-story pipeline. Output naturally flows into KB. Without this, research is ad-hoc and decisions lack durable evidence.
acceptance_conditions: A developer can run /momentum:research on a topic and receive a corpus-validated artifact with date-pinned citations that feeds into /momentum:decision.
momentum-deep-research-pipeline.notes: Base research skill shipped. Freshness/archival/date-anchoring and multi-model activation remain.

momentum-impetus-session-orientation.name: Impetus Session Orientation — Sprint State and Feature Status
momentum-impetus-session-orientation.description: The handoff between the developer's current context and the AI session. Impetus greets the developer at session start oriented to cycle position — which step of the Momentum cycle is active (triage → intake → feature-grooming → epic-grooming → refine → sprint-planning → sprint-dev → retro), current sprint state (stories in progress, blockers, fill bars), open journal threads, and a cached feature status summary with staleness flag. Developer arrives knowing exactly where the practice sits in the cycle.
momentum-impetus-session-orientation.value_analysis:
When a developer opens Claude Code mid-sprint, Impetus greets them with cycle position, sprint state, in-flight stories, and recent activity. This is one of the highest-value features in the current implementation — it is used every single session.

The pain this removes is real and daily: reconstructing 'where was I?' from files and git history at every session start. But the value goes beyond pain removal. Orientation is also about confidence — the developer knows the system is aware of its own state, and knows where in the practice cycle the work currently sits. That awareness makes the practice feel coherent rather than fragmented.

Currently fully working. All 4 orientation stories are done. The startup cache makes it fast.

Gaps: Cycle-position orientation (mapping session state to the named cycle step sequence from DEC-017) is not yet implemented — Impetus currently surfaces sprint state but does not explicitly name the active cycle step. The broader Impetus experience (personality, warmth) is a separate feature (momentum-impetus-experience).
momentum-impetus-session-orientation.system_context: The most-used feature in the practice. Orientation quality at session start determines whether the developer feels grounded or adrift. All other sprint execution features benefit from a developer who knows exactly where they are. Greeting consumes the cached feature-status summary silently at startup; the full canvas surface (momentum-canvas) is opened on demand for richer review of features, sprints, and findings.
acceptance_conditions: A developer opening a new Claude Code session with momentum:impetus receives a greeting that shows current sprint name, stories in progress vs done vs remaining, open journal threads (if any), and a feature status summary (cached, with staleness flag if features.json has changed since last cache).
momentum-impetus-session-orientation.notes: All 4 stories done as of sprint-2026-04-11. Feature is fully working. Personality layer is in momentum-impetus-experience. impetus-feature-status-cache lives here singly post-canvas-split (was previously double-listed under momentum-feature-status-visibility).

momentum-practice-knowledge-base.name: Practice Knowledge Base — Project-Local Cold Vault
momentum-practice-knowledge-base.description: Project-local cold knowledge vault powered by the installed Obsidian wiki skill suite (wiki-setup, wiki-ingest, wiki-research, wiki-synthesize, wiki-lint, wiki-rebuild, wiki-capture, wiki-update, wiki-history-ingest from github.com/Ar9av/obsidian-wiki; obsidian-cli, obsidian-markdown, obsidian-bases from github.com/kepano/obsidian-skills). Accessed via wiki-query as the standard Tier 3 cold KB interface. Custom kb-init/kb-ingest stories superseded by the installed skill suite per DEC-018.
momentum-practice-knowledge-base.value_analysis:
Current value: The installed Obsidian wiki skill suite is live and operational. The nornspun project vault (nornspun-agentic-kb) is active, with research, synthesis, and ingestion running. wiki-query provides Tier 3 cold KB access today — cited answers with [[wikilinks]] from a pre-synthesized wiki. The practice has moved from 'zero delivery' to 'infrastructure operational' without writing any custom stories.

Full vision: Every project using Momentum has a live Obsidian vault fed by wiki-ingest and wiki-research. Agents access it via wiki-query (both normal and fast/index-only modes). The wiki-query interface is baked into the hot constitution (Tier 1) so every agent everywhere has KB access without relying on per-workflow injection. Constitution.md includes the exact invocation syntax (per DEC-018 D2). Sprint-planning, build-guidelines, and retro all benefit from grounded, cited KB context. The practice becomes genuinely project-aware — not just pattern-aware from training data.

Known gaps: The wiki-query interface block is not yet in the hot constitution — agents without explicit workflow wiring cannot access the KB. The nornspun agent constitution (nornspun-agent-constitution-wiki-query-block) has not been updated. The superseded kb-init/kb-ingest stories need to be formally dropped (drop-superseded-kb-stories-dec-018).
momentum-practice-knowledge-base.system_context: Infrastructure feature. Feeds composable-specialist-agents (guideline generation benefits from cited KB context), deep-research-pipeline (research artifacts land here), and retro-flywheel accumulation. The installed skill suite provides ingestion, synthesis, lint, rebuild, and export — the full wiki lifecycle without custom development. wiki-query is the read interface; the vault is the store.
acceptance_conditions: A developer can run wiki-ingest against a project doc source and have the vault indexed, then invoke wiki-query to retrieve cited answers from wiki pages — and have the wiki-query interface block present in the project hot constitution so every agent has Tier 3 KB access without per-workflow wiring.
momentum-practice-knowledge-base.notes: Superseded stories (kb-init, kb-ingest, kb-raw-ingest-spike, vault-claudemd-navigation-contract-spec, vault-indexmd-registry-format-and-update-protocol, wiki-page-schema-and-frontmatter-formalization) replaced by installed wiki skill suite per DEC-018 (2026-05-03). Nornspun vault is live. wiki-query operational. Remaining work: constitution integration and formal story cleanup.

momentum-protocol-based-integration.name: Swappable Protocol Implementations
momentum-protocol-based-integration.description: Protocol abstraction layer enabling developers to swap AI providers, storage backends, or tool integrations without rewriting skill logic. Skills resolve tool calls through protocol interfaces defined in project configuration.
momentum-protocol-based-integration.value_analysis:
The Momentum practice is currently tightly coupled to Claude Code and Claude's API. A developer who wants to evaluate a different AI provider, swap storage backends, or integrate a different tool must rewrite skill logic to do so. This lock-in risk is not immediately painful — but it becomes critical when a vendor changes pricing, deprecates capabilities, or fails to meet a specific need.

The value here is resilience and optionality. A developer using a protocol-abstracted Momentum knows that their investment in skills, workflows, and practices is not hostage to any single vendor's decisions. They can adapt without rebuilding.

This is a future-facing value — less about current pain and more about protecting the value already delivered by other features.

Currently not started. No delivery. All 4 stories are backlog. This feature may be premature but is included to make the gap explicit.
momentum-protocol-based-integration.system_context: Future-proofing the platform layer. Without this, every other feature's value is partially at the mercy of vendor decisions. With it, the practice is genuinely portable.
acceptance_conditions: A developer can define an alternative protocol binding in project configuration and have skill tool calls resolve through that binding — without modifying the skill's workflow.md.
momentum-protocol-based-integration.notes: All stories backlog. No delivery yet. Included to make vendor lock-in gap explicit and tracked.

momentum-provenance-chain.name: Artifact Provenance and Staleness Detection
momentum-provenance-chain.description: Hash-based staleness detection and provenance chain for planning artifacts. Detects when a PRD, architecture doc, epic, or feature definition is out of sync with the implementation stories that reference it. Surfaces drift before it causes expensive rework.
momentum-provenance-chain.value_analysis:
Planning artifacts go stale. A PRD written six months ago may no longer reflect what was actually built. An architecture decision that got reversed may still appear as current. A developer working from stale specs builds the wrong thing — and often doesn't discover the mismatch until expensive rework is necessary.

Provenance detection gives the developer trust in the documentation system. When a planning artifact is marked 'verified as of X' and the code has changed significantly since then, the system surfaces the drift. The developer doesn't need to hold the spec-vs-code mapping in their head — the system tracks it.

This is not just pain removal but a new relationship with planning artifacts: they become living, monitored documents rather than static snapshots that are trusted until they suddenly aren't.

Currently not started. No delivery. All 3 stories are backlog.
momentum-provenance-chain.system_context: Quality guard for the entire artifact ecosystem. Without provenance, every planning artifact becomes a liability over time. With it, the trust in planning documents is explicit and monitored, making sprint planning more reliable.
acceptance_conditions: A developer can run a provenance scan and receive a report showing which planning artifacts are stale (hash drift detected since last verification) and which stories reference them — enabling targeted re-verification before sprint planning.
momentum-provenance-chain.notes: All stories backlog. No delivery yet. artifact-provenance epic has 2 additional stories assigned to assessment-decision-pipeline.

momentum-agent-role-contracts.name: Agent Role Contracts — Plugin-Shipped Base Bodies
momentum-agent-role-contracts.description: Plugin-shipped role definitions and base bodies for every agent role Momentum spawns. The role contract — what a 'dev' or 'qa-reviewer' or 'validator' is — without any project conditioning.
momentum-agent-role-contracts.value_analysis:
Current value: Plugin ships base bodies for dev, dev-build, dev-frontend, dev-skills, qa-reviewer, and e2e-validator. Sprint-dev reads these as system-prompt content when spawning. Architecture-guard exists as a real plugin agent (sonnet, context:fork, allowed-tools Read+Glob+Grep+Bash). AVFL's validator/enumerator/adversary/consolidator/fixer ship as sub-skills inside avfl/sub-skills/. The hand-rolled frontend-dev skill is the working existence proof of the role-contract shape.

Full vision: Per DEC-013 (universal agent model) and DEC-016 (two-tier taxonomy), every named agent role Momentum spawns has a plugin-shipped definition file. Tier B shipped customs — validator, enumerator, adversary, retro auditor-human/execution/review, documenter, fixer, code-reviewer, architect-guard, dev-fixer — are concrete: the base body IS the production definition. Tier A bases — architect, pm, sm, plus the project-conditioned cases of code-reviewer/architect-guard/dev-fixer — define the role contract that the agent-composition-pipeline layers project rules onto. Sprint-dev and retro spawn from a complete role catalog with no missing definitions.

Known gaps: 11 Tier B agent-definition stories backlog (some likely superseded by AVFL sub-skills — to resolve in epic-grooming). 7 base-body stories backlog (architect/pm/sm/etc.). Cull/consolidation questions open: dev-fixer = dev with scoped prompt? AVFL roles already as sub-skills make separate plugin agent files redundant? Retro synthesizer a typed role or inline writer? These resolve in the next epic-grooming pass.
momentum-agent-role-contracts.system_context: The agent vocabulary layer. Every other spawning skill (sprint-dev, retro, avfl, distill, quick-fix, sprint-planning) reads from this catalog. Without it, every spawn either invents a role inline or fails to spawn at all.
acceptance_conditions: A developer can run sprint-dev or retro and observe that every named agent role is spawned from a plugin-shipped definition file (or sub-skill), with no workflow attempting to spawn an undefined role.
momentum-agent-role-contracts.notes: Split out from momentum-composable-specialist-agents on 2026-05-14. Several agent-definition stories may be culled in epic-grooming (AVFL roles superseded by sub-skills; dev-fixer may unify with dev).

momentum-agent-spawn-orchestration.name: Agent Spawn Orchestration — Preflight, Observability, Pattern Enforcement
momentum-agent-spawn-orchestration.description: How Momentum spawns agents reliably: preflight verification, observability, state verification, parallel-spawn enforcement, and prompt-shape audits.
momentum-agent-spawn-orchestration.value_analysis:
Current value: Sprint-dev and other spawners declare spawn patterns in workflow team-composition blocks. The mandatory `spawning: individual-agent | team-create` field plus `concurrency: parallel | sequential` makes intent explicit. The global spawning-patterns.md rule distinguishes fan-out from TeamCreate. guidelines-verification-gate is in place.

Full vision: Every spawn is verifiable. Preflight checks agent definition exists and prompt is structurally valid (large-file guidance present, no missing required slots). Observability captures spawn count, duration, model, token usage per call. Post-spawn state verification confirms artifact production. Parallel-spawn enforcement detects accidental serialization. Specific known failure modes — fan-out prompt fix, SendMessage self-routing filter, read-only investigation, Explore directory hints, dev-as-executor-not-decider — are closed with targeted prompt audits.

Known gaps: Preflight, observability metric, state-verification hook, and parallel-spawn enforcement are all backlog. The specific prompt-fix stories address real, observed failure modes from prior sprints — they're not speculative.
momentum-agent-spawn-orchestration.system_context: The reliability layer for every multi-agent workflow. Pairs with agent-role-contracts (which defines what gets spawned) and agent-composition-pipeline (which defines what the spawned agent knows). Without spawn-orchestration, sprint-dev and retro pay an opaque tax in mis-spawns and prompt drift.
acceptance_conditions: A developer can run sprint-dev and observe an observability log entry for every spawn, with a preflight check that halts spawning when an agent definition is missing.

momentum-gherkin-separation.name: Black-Box Behavioral Specification via Gherkin
momentum-gherkin-separation.description: Behavioral specification of skills via Gherkin feature files that are independent of implementation. E2E validator checks behavior from the outside. Skill implementations can change without modifying specs.
momentum-gherkin-separation.value_analysis:
Gherkin specs describe behavior from the outside — what the system does, not how it does it. This separation has compounding value: a developer can change a skill's implementation completely without touching the spec. An AI agent can validate behavior objectively without reading implementation code.

The new capability this unlocks is independent validation. An E2E validator that checks behavior from the outside can catch regressions that no amount of internal code review would surface — because it tests what the user experiences, not what the code says. This is a qualitatively different kind of test coverage.

Currently working: Gherkin specs exist for many skills. The behavioral spec / implementation separation principle is established and the E2E validator uses these specs.

Gaps: AVFL checkpoint preview integration is remaining. Story-spec completeness checklist not yet implemented.
momentum-gherkin-separation.system_context: Enables independent validation agents to verify behavior objectively. Without Gherkin separation, validation is always tightly coupled to implementation — and breaks whenever implementation changes. Makes quality-gates-enforced more reliable.
acceptance_conditions: A developer can write a Gherkin spec for a skill that is validated by the E2E validator without the validator reading the skill's workflow.md — testing behavior, not implementation.
momentum-gherkin-separation.notes: Gherkin workflow active (gherkin-acs-and-atdd-workflow-active counted in sprint-planning-to-ready). Remaining: AVFL checkpoint preview integration and story-spec completeness checklist.

momentum-model-routing-strategy.name: Model and Effort Routing Defaults
momentum-model-routing-strategy.description: Frontmatter-driven model tier selection for agent spawns. Skill SKILL.md files declare effort level (quick/medium/high); the practice routes agent spawns to the appropriate model tier automatically.
momentum-model-routing-strategy.value_analysis:
Every agent spawn involves an implicit decision: which model, which effort tier. Without routing defaults, the developer either overthinks every spawn or defaults to the most capable (most expensive) model uniformly. Both are bad outcomes — one wastes cognitive effort, the other wastes money.

Model routing makes this decision automatic: frontmatter effort declarations translate to model tiers, and the practice routes accordingly. The developer declares how hard a task is (quick/medium/high), not which model to use. This is both cognitive simplification and cost discipline.

The deeper value is that it makes large sprints economically feasible. Running 20 haiku-effort agents at haiku prices is what enables ambitious parallelism without unsustainable API costs.

Currently working: model routing via frontmatter is implemented and active across all skills.

Gaps: Benchmark validation that routing produces the right quality-vs-cost tradeoffs has not been implemented. The routing works but its accuracy hasn't been formally measured.
momentum-model-routing-strategy.system_context: Makes the whole platform economically viable at scale. Without routing, either cost spirals or quality suffers. With it, the practice can grow in ambition without corresponding cost growth.
acceptance_conditions: A developer can declare effort: quick in a SKILL.md and have all agent spawns from that skill route to haiku without any additional configuration — and can verify with a benchmark run that the routing matches cost expectations.
momentum-model-routing-strategy.notes: Routing via frontmatter working. Benchmark validation stories (performance-validation epic) backlog.

momentum-quality-gates-enforced.name: Quality Gates — AVFL, Code Review, and Retro Applied to Every Sprint
momentum-quality-gates-enforced.description: The quality gate integration across the sprint lifecycle. Every story goes through AVFL validation (adversarial validate-fix loop), concurrent code review, and E2E validation before merge. Gate failures block merge until resolved.
momentum-quality-gates-enforced.value_analysis:
Quality gates change the developer's relationship with defects. Without them, defects are discovered after merge, sometimes after demo. With automated gates, defects surface before they compound. The developer gets immediate feedback when a story breaks its acceptance criteria.

But the deeper value is the psychological safety it creates. A developer who knows that every merge is gated can move faster — because they know the system will catch what they miss. The gates are not bureaucratic checkpoints; they are the reason confident, rapid development is possible.

Currently working: AVFL and team review gates run in every sprint. The practice catches real issues. The developer doesn't need to manually review every merge.

Gaps: The AVFL fixer (automated remediation for common defects) is not yet implemented. Some gate failures require manual analysis and fixing. Known defect escapes exist in practice.
momentum-quality-gates-enforced.system_context: The enforcement layer that makes all other features trustworthy. Without gates, any feature can regress silently. With them, 'working software at sprint end' is the default expectation, not the optimistic one.
acceptance_conditions: A sprint completes with: every merged story having passed AVFL + code review + E2E validation; the retro producing a findings document from transcript audit; gate failures reported with actionable resolution guidance.
momentum-quality-gates-enforced.notes: Gate infrastructure working. AVFL fixer and default agent composition backlog. Known escapes exist — hence partial.

momentum-startup-performance.name: Sub-2s Session Startup (NFR20)
momentum-startup-performance.description: Session startup performance target: Impetus greeting fully rendered in under 2 seconds from command to ready state. Achieved via startup preflight caching, IO consolidation, and deferred writes.
momentum-startup-performance.value_analysis:
Session startup latency is a silent tax. A 5-second greeting is not noticed in isolation — but across hundreds of sessions per month, it accumulates, and worse, it sets the tone. A session that starts slowly feels heavy before any work is done.

The full vision is that startup is imperceptible — the developer types a command and is immediately in context, with no noticeable wait. Under 2 seconds means the session starts before the developer notices they're waiting. This is not just a performance metric — it is an experience metric that shapes how the practice feels daily.

The preflight cache (just shipped in sprint-2026-04-11) significantly improves the happy path. For a cached, up-to-date session, startup is now fast.

Gaps: IO consolidation (install/upgrade path) is remaining. Cold-start and cache-miss paths may still be slow. The 2s target is not yet formally verified across all paths.
momentum-startup-performance.system_context: First-impression quality. Impetus is the most-used feature and a slow startup makes everything downstream feel heavier. Startup performance amplifies the value of every session-start feature by ensuring they are experienced without friction.
acceptance_conditions: A developer running momentum:impetus in a cached state (no hash drift) receives the full greeting — sprint state, feature status, menu — in under 2 seconds on a standard developer machine.
momentum-startup-performance.notes: Preflight cache (impetus-feature-status-cache) is the impetus-session-orientation cache mechanism. Silent-preflight done. Install-upgrade IO consolidation backlog.

ad-hoc.name: Ad-Hoc Work
ad-hoc.description: Canonical residue catcher. Accepts stories that don't belong to any specific finite-lived epic — quick fixes, tactical work, sprint-specific tasks, and stories that haven't been assigned to a deliverable epic yet.
ad-hoc.notes: Kept as long-lived per DEC-034 D2 and D5 explicit language. The canonical residue catcher: every sprint produces some ad-hoc work that doesn't fit a deliverable shape. Forcing it into a finite-lived epic would create artificial epics. Long-lived with no closure expectation.

_migration.notes: This _migration key is reserved provenance metadata. It is not an epic entry. Tooling that iterates epics.json must skip this key.
